content.assessment: This incremental compromise is explicitly designed to reduce political conflict scope relative to full abolition, targeting only the earliest and most controversial entry point. This design logic is consistent with the pattern observed where narrower reforms attract sharper reframing than intended, so while the proposal aims for lower opposition intensity, the precedent evidence suggests even partial reform can be escalated rhetorically by opposition actors.
opportunities: Could serve as a test case to gauge coalition and public reaction before pursuing more extensive reform, if it manages to avoid the escalation pattern seen elsewhere
strengths: Deliberately narrows political conflict scope by targeting only one entry point rather than the full track structure, an explicit attempt to avoid the sweeping-transformation framing triggered by broader proposals [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
threats: Church-school and middle-class veto constituencies, having already required explicit reassurance against any perceived move toward abolition, may resist even this partial phase-down as a precedent-setting threat [F-live-political_feasibility-05]
weaknesses: Precedent shows that even narrowly scoped proposals have been reframed by opposition as evidence of a larger structural agenda, undermining the assumption that partial reform reduces opposition intensity [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
